 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-hhjnp","title":"fcp-audit: verify_chain accepts arbitrarily-future timestamps (no upper-bound sanity)","description":"## Finding
verify_chain (crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:1081-1095) detects backward timestamp regressions: `if entry.occurred_at < prev.occurred_at { issues.push(... "audit.timestamp_regression" ...) }`. It accepts any forward-dated timestamp, including absurd futures (year 2096+ or u64::MAX).

## Consequences
1. **Freshness SLA poisoning.** FreshnessLevel + AuditStatus (lib.rs:1226-1302) compute freshness from the head timestamp. A single forged future-dated entry renders the chain "fresh" forever.
2. **Audit-query time-range poisoning.** Time-range filters return unexpected results.
3. **Monotonicity is preserved but meaningless.** An attacker who can inject one future entry can append arbitrarily many more with same-or-later timestamps and pass the regression check.

## Why this is a missing invariant
The current check establishes a lower bound (non-decreasing) but no upper bound. Calendar times outside a plausible window should be rejected. The verifier has no wall-clock input today.

## Recommendation
Add an optional `now_ceiling: Option<u64>` parameter (or a VerifyOptions struct) to verify_chain. When Some(now), reject entries with `occurred_at > now + clock_skew_grace` (e.g., 300 seconds). Emit "audit.timestamp_future" as a critical VerifyIssue. Leave None for offline audit replays with no wall clock.

Alternative minimum: add a static hard cap (`FUTURE_TIMESTAMP_HARD_CAP = 4_000_000_000` = year 2096) and reject anything beyond that statically. Crude but catches absurd cases.

## Evidence
- crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:1081-1095 (regression check only)
- crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:1226-1302 (FreshnessLevel / AuditStatus consumers)
- No future-dated-entry rejection test exists (grep of lib.rs + tests/ for "timestamp_future" / "future_timestamp" returns zero hits).","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:47:44.584375Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T05:33:58.012923Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T05:33:58.011273Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2808,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-hhjnp","author":"jemanuel","text":"Fixed in 480020af. Added MAX_FUTURE_TIMESTAMP_SKEW_SECS=300 (Kerberos convention) and verify_chain_with_clock(entries, head, zone_id, now). Flags audit.timestamp_future as critical when occurred_at > now+skew. Uses saturating_add so u64::MAX is handled. Offline replays keep verify_chain. 368 lib tests pass; 4 new tests cover far-future, within-skew boundary, u64::MAX, and clean-chain agreement.","created_at":"2026-04-21T05:33:57Z"}]}

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-hkhy","title":"[IDEA-17] Schema Hash + Canonical CBOR Vector Generator","description":"## Background
Spec and docs require deterministic CBOR + schema hash vectors, but maintaining them manually is error-prone. We need a generator to keep vectors and schema hashes in sync with the authoritative definitions.

## Goals
- Generate schema hashes and canonical CBOR vectors from source schemas.
- Emit stable JSON/CBOR fixtures for conformance tests.
- Integrate with CI to detect drift.

## Non-goals
- Auto-modify spec text or accept non-canonical encodings.

## Approach
- Add a generator tool in `fcp-conformance` (CLI subcommand).
- Parse schema definitions (CDDL/JSON schema or Rust type registry).
- Compute schema hash via existing canonicalization routines.
- Write vector files with deterministic ordering.

## Risks
- Schema evolution could break determinism; add fixtures and strict ordering rules.

## Success Criteria
- Generator produces identical outputs across runs/platforms.
- CI can regenerate and diff vectors without noise.

## Acceptance Criteria (Refined)
- Regenerated vectors are byte‑for‑byte stable across two consecutive runs.
- Schema hash changes when a schema field definition changes.
- CI diff check fails on any vector drift.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-l25","title":"fcp.terraform: Resource import for existing infrastructure","description":"# fcp.terraform: Resource import for existing infrastructure (FCP2)

## Goal
Allow controlled `terraform import` so agents can bring existing infrastructure under state management.

Import mutates state and can be risky; require approval gating.

## Scope
- Import a resource into state with explicit address + provider id.
- Validate inputs and prevent injection into CLI arguments.
- Emit receipts/audit for the import action.

## Capability model (illustrative)
- `terraform.import` (dangerous)

## FCP2 requirements
- Single-zone binding.
- Sandbox profile restricts filesystem + process execution.
- NetworkConstraints default deny unless provider APIs are explicitly allowed.
- Approval gating recommended.

## Deliverables
- Operation schema:
  - `terraform.import`
- Input validation:
  - strict address grammar
  - strict provider id format

## Test strategy (MUST)
- Unit tests with a terraform shim for:
  - arg construction
  - approval gating
  - audit/receipt emission

## Acceptance criteria
- Imports are mechanically authorized, safe, and auditable.

- All parsing paths have test coverage
- DoS protections verified
- Golden vectors match exactly

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-la0l1","title":"fcp-audit: AuditEntry has no signer binding (authenticity gap at crate boundary)","description":"## Finding
`AuditEntry` (crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:152) carries `actor: String` but has no signer key, no signature, no issuer attestation field. `computed_id` (lib.rs:234) is a blake3 hash of the canonical payload (integrity-against-edit, not authenticity). `verify_chain` performs no signer check.

Consequences:
1. **Forgery by any entity with append access.** Anyone who can append to the store can produce an entry claiming `actor = "agent:alice"` with no way for a verifier to refute it using the audit crate alone.
2. **Hash linkage only prevents after-the-fact tampering.** A fresh chain assembled from forged entries passes `verify_chain` cleanly: seqs are monotonic, prev pointers match, zone IDs align.
3. **`event_types::AUDIT_FORK_DETECTED` handling is incomplete.** `verify_chain` detects `audit.fork_detected` (two entries at same seq with different ids) but provides no policy — a verifier cannot tell which fork is authoritative without out-of-band signer info.

## Why this is underspecified
The crate-level rustdoc (lib.rs:1-5) says "protocol-level types for audit chains, decision receipts, event filtering, and chain verification." It does not state that authenticity is out of scope and must be enforced by a wrapping layer. A reader (or agent) building against fcp-audit can plausibly conclude that `verify_chain` is a full trust root — it is not.

## Recommendation (choose one)
- **Option A (strong):** Add `issuer_kid: KeyId` + `signature: Ed25519Signature` fields to `AuditEntry`. Have `verify_chain` accept a `key_lookup: impl Fn(&KeyId) -> Option<Ed25519VerifyingKey>` and verify each entry. Requires depending on fcp-crypto.
- **Option B (docs + fork policy):** Add crate-level rustdoc explicitly stating: "AuditEntry authenticity is out of scope for this crate. Callers MUST wrap entries with issuer signatures before persisting; `verify_chain` only verifies integrity against edits, not authenticity against forgery." Add a `ForkPolicy` enum to `VerifyReport` so fork-resolution semantics are explicit.

Option B is the viable minimum; Option A is the principled fix if authenticity should live in the crate.

## Evidence
- crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:152-185 (AuditEntry: no signature field)
- crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:234-261 (computed_id: blake3 hash, not signature)
- crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:927-1158 (verify_chain: no signer lookup)
- crates/fcp-audit/src/lib.rs:1-5 (crate docs do not disclose the gap)
- crates/fcp-audit/Cargo.toml:14-21 (no fcp-crypto dep confirming gap is architectural)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-21T04:47:37.678875Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-21T05:19:52.063018Z","closed_at":"2026-04-21T05:19:51.453377Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"comments":[{"id":2805,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-la0l1","author":"jemanuel","text":"Shipped in commit bef626cf.  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-qvzvp","title":"[MEDIUM] SymbolAck/DecodeStatus replay: signed transcripts lack recipient binding","description":"SymbolAck::transcript_bytes (fcps.rs:748-763) and DecodeStatus::transcript_bytes (fcps.rs:606-634) both include epoch_id (cross-epoch replay protection) but neither binds the recipient NodeId or a per-request nonce.

Within a single epoch, an attacker who observes a valid signed SymbolAck from B addressed to A can replay it to a third recipient D, provided D also has a pending request for the same object_id from B in the same epoch. On D, handle_symbol_ack (node.rs:1416) calls sent_symbols.remove(&ack.object_id), prematurely cancelling D's outbound transfer. Same vector for DecodeStatus via process_decode_status.

Recommended fix: extend SymbolAck and DecodeStatus to carry a `to: NodeId` field (or a per-request nonce echoed from the original SymbolRequest) and include it in the signing transcript. Bump transcript domain separators to V2 to prevent cross-version replay.

Discovered during code review of fcp-mesh after the Apr 19 swarm closed the four-handler signature-verification gap (81f303c8). The signature-verification work itself is correct; this is a residual transcript-binding gap exposed by that hardening.

Severity: MEDIUM. Requires multi-recipient request fan-out in the same epoch to exploit, and the consequence is transfer cancellation (availability), not data exfiltration.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-20T07:06:43.709405Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-20T07:18:54.488352Z","closed_at":"2026-04-20T07:18:54.488054Z","close_reason":"Bound SymbolAck/DecodeStatus V2 transcripts to recipient node and request nonce","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

The fix is simple: define ONE zone-wide precedence that all operators follow. WHERE: crates/fwc/src/truth.rs — add TruthPrecedencePolicy struct loaded from zone configuration. (4) At least 4 tests: same-zone consistency, policy override for specific zone, fallback when higher-precedence source unavailable, serialization round-trip. ESTIMATED EFFORT: 2-3 days.  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-y1q8","title":"Connector Lifecycle Management (Activate/Update/Rollback)","description":"# [FCP2] Connector Lifecycle Management (Activate / Update / Rollback)

## Goal
Implement connector lifecycle management as specified in Spec V2 §14.1–14.2:
- secure activation (sandbox + policy + capability gating)
- staged updates
- crash-loop detection
- rollback / pinning

This bead is **host/orchestrator** behavior (MeshNode / `fcp-host`), not connector-specific code.

## Why This Matters
Connectors are security-critical binaries that execute with zone-scoped authority. Lifecycle management is where we:
- prevent “unsigned code ran” incidents
- enforce sandbox defaults *before* first byte of connector code executes
- provide safe operations ergonomics (predictable rollback, explainable failures)

## Hard Requirements (V2-only)
- No FCP1 compatibility paths.
- Single-zone binding per connector instance.
- Default deny: a connector cannot perform an operation without a valid CapabilityToken.
- Fail closed on ambiguous outcomes (esp. during update/rollback).

## Normative Alignment (Spec §14)
### §14.1 Activation Requirements
On activation, MeshNode MUST ensure (in order):
1. **Supply-chain verification**: manifest+binary verification chain passes (see `flywheel_connectors-1n78.27`).
2. **Policy ceilings**: requested capabilities/sandbox/network constraints are within zone ceilings (see `flywheel_connectors-1n78.26`).
3. **Sandbox creation**: OS/WASI sandbox instantiated from manifest profile (see `flywheel_connectors-1n78.18`).
4. **Credential injection discipline**: secrets never touch disk; secretless-by-default egress injection preferred (see `flywheel_connectors-kt9r`).
5. **Handshake + readiness**: connector standard method surface is reachable and stable (`handshake`, `health`, etc.).
6. **Audit**: activation emits an audit event with evidence (manifest id, binary id, policy heads, zone checkpoint).

### §14.2 Updates and Rollback
- Updates MUST be verifiable and staged (not “swap binary in place blindly”).
- Crash loops MUST trigger a safe fallback.
- Owner can pin a known-good version.

## Design / Deliverables
### A) Connector lifecycle state machine
A host-visible state machine that drives deterministic behavior:

- NotInstalled
- Installed (verified artifacts present)
- Activating (phased)
- Running (healthy)
- Unhealthy (recoverable)
- Failed (crash-loop / policy refusal)
- RolledBack

### B) Activation workflow
Activation is phased to make failures diagnosable:
1. Resolve requested connector version and retrieve artifacts (registry or mesh mirror).
2. Verify manifest/binary + policy requirements.
3. Build sandbox profile (fs/network/time/mem/cpu limits) and start connector.
4. Perform standard method checks:
   - `--manifest` extractable without execution
   - `handshake` succeeds
   - `describe`/`introspect` callable
5. Register connector instance with the node runtime (zone-bound).
6. Start health monitor.

### C) Update manager
- Staged rollout strategy:
  - canary → expand → full
  - per-zone policy may require manual approval for risky connectors
- Version pinning:
  - per connector + zone
  - explicit reason + audit event

### D) Crash-loop detection + rollback
CrashLoopDetector:
- detects N crashes in M seconds
- emits audit event with evidence
- rolls back to last-known-good version if available
- fails closed if no verified previous version exists

Rollback must preserve:
- revocation enforcement
- policy ceilings
- audit trail

### E) Observability and error mapping
- All lifecycle failures map to FCP error taxonomy (`flywheel_connectors-1n78.29`).
- Structured logs include:
  - connector_id, zone_id, version
  - binary_id + manifest_id
  - phase + reason_code

## Tests
- Unit tests: state machine transitions and crash-loop detection.
- Integration tests: staged update and rollback in deterministic harness.
- Adversarial tests:
  - attempt to activate without verification → denied
  - attempt to activate with policy-incompatible sandbox/network → denied
  - crash-loop triggers rollback deterministically

## Acceptance Criteria
- Activation fails closed unless verification + policy checks succeed.
- Rollback is deterministic and auditable.
- Crash-loop behavior is tested and produces actionable logs.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-y54mi","title":"fcp-streaming: websocket reconnect path allows zero-delay retry storms","description":"crates/fcp-streaming/src/reconnect.rs:63-64 sets initial_delay without a floor, crates/fcp-streaming/src/reconnect.rs:191 uses that raw delay in wait_for_reconnect(), and crates/fcp-streaming/src/websocket.rs:673-679 feeds client.config.reconnect_delay straight into ReconnectConfig. A zero reconnect_delay collapses the backoff sleep to Duration::ZERO and turns repeated handshake failures into a hot reconnect loop. Excerpt: reconnect.rs:63-64 pub const fn with_initial_delay(mut self, delay: Duration) -> Self { self.initial_delay = delay; } reconnect.rs:191 let delay = self.config.delay_for_attempt(self.attempts); websocket.rs:673-679 let config = crate::reconnect::ReconnectConfig::new() ... .with_initial_delay(client.config.reconnect_delay);","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-22T11:08:41.502275Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-04-22T11:17:47.125867Z","closed_at":"2026-04-22T11:17:47.125547Z","close_reason":"Added MIN_RECONNECT_DELAY=100ms floor and tightened MAX_RECONNECT_DELAY from 60s→30s + jitter from ±50% to ±20% in delay_for_attempt. Zero or sub-ms initial_delay is now rounded up to 100ms, defeating hot retry storm. Two new async regression tests drive real wall-clock waits: test_rapid_reconnect_waits_are_floored_by_min_delay (zero initial_delay → every wait ≥ 100ms) and test_rapid_reconnect_waits_are_monotonically_increasing (growing schedule with cap hit). Full suite: 511 lib + 81 integration passing.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
